symptoms.description: The patient frequently consumes the substance in larger amounts or over a longer period than originally intended.
The patient expresses a persistent desire or makes unsuccessful efforts to cut down or control their substance use.
The patient spends a great deal of time in activities necessary to obtain the substance, use it, or recover from its effects.
The patient experiences an intense craving or a strong urge to use the specific substance.
The patient engages in recurrent substance use that results in a failure to fulfill major role obligations at work, school, or home.
The patient experiences significant interpersonal problems, withdrawal from social or recreational activities, or failure to fulfill major role obligations due to substance use.
The patient gives up or significantly reduces important social, occupational, or recreational activities due to substance use.
The patient repeatedly uses the substance in physically hazardous situations or continues use despite knowing it causes ongoing physical or psychological harm.
The patient continues to use the substance despite knowing it causes or exacerbates a persistent physical or psychological problem.
The patient develops tolerance, requiring markedly increased amounts of the substance to achieve the desired effect, or experiencing diminished effects with the same amount.
The patient experiences characteristic withdrawal symptoms when the substance is discontinued, or takes the substance to relieve or avoid withdrawal symptoms.

threshold_count: 1 or more of 6 physical/neurological signs plus maladaptive behavioral changes

symptoms.description: The patient exhibits clinically significant problematic behavioral or psychological changes, such as inappropriate sexual or aggressive behavior, mood lability, or impaired judgment, during or shortly after alcohol ingestion.
The patient exhibits slurred, unclear speech shortly after alcohol ingestion.
The patient exhibits impaired coordination during or shortly after alcohol ingestion.
The patient exhibits an unsteady gait and poor coordination shortly after alcohol ingestion.
The patient exhibits nystagmus, or jerking eye movements, shortly after alcohol ingestion.
The patient exhibits an impairment in attention or memory shortly after alcohol ingestion.
The patient exhibits stupor or coma during or shortly after alcohol ingestion.
The patient experiences clinically important distress or impairment in social, occupational, or other important areas of functioning due to the symptoms.

duration_rule: Hours to days after stopping heavy, lengthy use

symptoms.description: The patient exhibits autonomic hyperactivity, such as sweating or a rapid pulse, during substance withdrawal.

The patient exhibits an involuntary trembling or shaking, presenting as a symptom of central nervous system overactivity following the abrupt cessation or reduction of heavy, prolonged alcohol or substance consumption.
The patient experiences insomnia or other significant sleep disturbances.
The patient experiences nausea or vomiting as a symptom of substance withdrawal.
The patient experiences transient visual, tactile, or auditory hallucinations or illusions during substance withdrawal.
The patient exhibits psychomotor agitation, such as pacing, hand-wringing, or an inability to sit still.
The patient experiences significant anxiety as a symptom of substance withdrawal.
The patient experiences generalized tonic-clonic seizures as a severe manifestation of alcohol or substance withdrawal.
The patient experiences clinically important distress or impairment in social, occupational, or other important areas of functioning due to the symptoms.

threshold_count: 5 or more of 12 symptoms following high-dose consumption (typically 250mg+)

symptoms.description: The patient experiences restlessness following caffeine consumption.
The patient experiences nervousness following caffeine consumption.
The patient exhibits excitement following caffeine consumption.
The patient experiences insomnia following caffeine consumption.
The patient exhibits a flushed face following caffeine consumption.
The patient experiences diuresis following caffeine consumption.
The patient experiences gastrointestinal disturbance following caffeine consumption.
The patient experiences muscle twitching shortly after consuming an excessive amount of caffeine.
The patient exhibits a rambling flow of thought and speech shortly after consuming caffeine.
The patient experiences tachycardia or cardiac arrhythmia following caffeine consumption.
The patient experiences periods of inexhaustibility following caffeine consumption.
The patient exhibits psychomotor agitation following caffeine consumption.
The patient experiences clinically important distress or impairment in social, occupational, or other important areas of functioning due to the symptoms.

threshold_count: 3 or more of 5 symptoms following abrupt cessation of daily use

symptoms.description: The patient experiences headaches as a symptom of withdrawal after abruptly stopping or reducing caffeine intake.
The patient experiences marked fatigue or drowsiness following the abrupt cessation or reduction of caffeine intake.
The patient experiences a depressed mood or irritability following the abrupt cessation or reduction of caffeine intake.
The patient experiences difficulty concentrating following the abrupt cessation or reduction of caffeine intake.
The patient experiences flu-like symptoms following the abrupt cessation or reduction of caffeine intake.
The patient experiences clinically important distress or impairment in social, occupational, or other important areas of functioning due to the symptoms.
The patient needs to gamble with increasing amounts of money in order to achieve the desired excitement.
The patient feels restless or irritable when attempting to cut down or stop gambling.
The patient has made repeated, unsuccessful efforts to control, cut back, or stop gambling.
The patient is often preoccupied with gambling, such as reliving past gambling experiences, planning the next venture, or thinking of ways to get money to gamble.
The patient often gambles when feeling distressed, such as when feeling helpless, guilty, anxious, or depressed.
The patient frequently chases their losses by returning another day to gamble more in order to get even.
The patient lies to conceal the extent of their involvement with gambling.
The patient has jeopardized or lost a significant relationship, job, or educational or career opportunity because of gambling.
The patient relies on others to provide money to relieve desperate financial situations caused by gambling.
The patient experiences clinically important distress or impairment in social, occupational, or other important areas of functioning due to the symptoms.
